1: Listen to me, you who pursue righteousness, you who seek the LORD: look to the rock from which you were hewn, and to the quarry from which you were dug. The call to look backward to origins — to Abraham and Sarah from whom YHWH carved the covenant people. The NT applies the Abraham-pattern to Christ's community: Romans 4:16-17 — Abraham is the father of us all — of all who share his faith. Galatians 3:16: the promises were made to Abraham and to his offspring... who is Christ. The rock from which believers are hewn is Abraham; but behind Abraham stands Christ, in whom all the Abrahamic promises are yes and amen (2 Corinthians 1:20).
2: Look to Abraham your father and to Sarah who bore you; for he was but one when I called him, that I might bless him and multiply him. One called and blessed to become many — the pattern of election and multiplication that the NT applies to Christ's work. Galatians 3:29: if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise. The one-called-to-multiply is also the pattern of the grain of wheat: John 12:24 — unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. The fruitful multiplication of the covenant community comes through death-and-resurrection, as Isaac's birth came through the as-good-as-dead bodies of Abraham and Sarah (Hebrews 11:12).
3: For the LORD will comfort Zion; he will comfort all her waste places and will make her wilderness like Eden, her desert like the garden of the LORD; joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the voice of song. The Eden-restoration — wilderness becoming paradise. Revelation 22:1-5 presents the new Jerusalem explicitly as a restored Eden: the river of life, the tree of life, no more curse. Christ as the new Adam (Romans 5:14-19; 1 Corinthians 15:45) through whom what Adam lost is fully restored. The comfort of Zion is the Spirit whom Christ sends: John 14:16-18 — the Paraclete (Comforter) who creates the community of joy, gladness, and thanksgiving.
4: Give attention to me, my people, and give ear to me, my nation; for a law will go out from me, and I will set my justice for a light to the peoples. The law and light going out to the peoples — the universal mission of the covenant community. John 8:12: I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness. Matthew 5:17: I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them. The Sermon on the Mount is the law going out from the Messianic hill (Matthew 5:1) as the universal charter of the kingdom — and Christ himself is the light that goes out to the peoples (John 1:9: the true light, which gives light to everyone).
5: My righteousness draws near, my salvation has gone out, and my arms will judge the peoples; the coastlands hope for me, and for my arm they wait. The arm of YHWH bringing salvation — and the Gentile coastlands waiting for it. Matthew 12:21 quotes Isaiah 42:4 — and in his name the Gentiles will hope — as the summary of Jesus's mission. Isaiah 53:1 asks: to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? — and answers: in the Servant's suffering. The arm that judges and saves is Christ at the right hand of the Father (Acts 2:33): the risen Christ who poured out the Spirit and who will return to complete the judgment of the peoples.
6: Lift up your eyes to the heavens, and look at the earth beneath; for the heavens vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, and they who dwell in it will die in like manner; but my salvation will be forever, and my righteousness will never be dismayed. The permanent salvation contrasted with the perishing creation. Hebrews 1:10-12 applies the wearing-out-like-a-garment image directly to the Son: You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning... they will perish, but you remain; they will all wear out like a garment... but you are the same, and your years will have no end. 1 Peter 1:24-25: all flesh is grass, but the word of the Lord — the gospel — endures forever. Christ is both the eternal Son (who outlasts the creation) and the eternal salvation (who never ends).
7: Listen to me, you who know righteousness, the people in whose heart is my law; fear not the reproach of man, nor be dismayed at their revilings. The people whose hearts have the law — the definitive mark of the new covenant community. Jeremiah 31:33: I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts. Hebrews 8:10 quotes this as the new covenant's central promise, fulfilled in the Spirit Christ sends. The freedom from fear of human reproach is the freedom of justification: Romans 8:1 — there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. Those who know righteousness (in Christ, 2 Corinthians 5:21) are no longer dismayed by human opinion.
8: For the moth will eat them up like a garment, and the worm will eat them like wool; but my righteousness will be forever, and my salvation to all generations. The eternal righteousness against the moth-eaten opposition — the same image as Isaiah 50:9 (the Servant's accusers who wear out while he endures). Romans 1:16-17: I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes... in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith for faith. The righteousness that endures to all generations is not a human achievement but the righteousness of God — the salvation accomplished at the cross that no opposition can ultimately threaten.
9: Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of the LORD; awake, as in days of old, the generations of long ago. Was it not you who cut Rahab in pieces, who pierced the dragon? The appeal for the arm of YHWH to repeat the Exodus-miracle that split the chaos-sea-dragon. Isaiah 53:1 asks to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? — answered in the Servant's suffering. Revelation 12:9: the great dragon — that ancient serpent who is the devil — is thrown down at the cross by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony (Revelation 12:11). Colossians 2:15: Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him. The arm that pierced Rahab pierces the last enemy at the resurrection.
10: Was it not you who dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep, who made the depths of the sea a way for the redeemed to pass over? The Exodus-path through the sea as the type of redemption. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4: our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea... and all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ. The way through the sea is the way Christ makes through death — the pattern of the resurrection. Hebrews 11:29: the Israelites passed through the Red Sea; Christ passes through the sea of death and opens a new and living way for his people (Hebrews 10:20).
11: And the ransomed of the LORD shall return and come to Zion with singing; everlasting joy shall be upon their heads; they shall obtain gladness and joy, and sorrow and sighing shall flee away. This verse echoes Isaiah 35:10 almost verbatim — the joy of the ransomed reaching Zion. Revelation 5:9: you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation. The ransom is Christ himself: Matthew 20:28 — the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many. The everlasting joy with sorrow and sighing fled away is the eschatological state described in Revelation 21:4: he will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more.
12: I, I am he who comforts you; who are you that you are afraid of man who dies, of the son of man who is made like grass? YHWH as the sole Comforter — and the absurdity of fearing mortal man while forgetting him. John 14:16-18: Jesus promises the Paraclete (Comforter/Advocate) and says I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4: the God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction, so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction. The fear of dying men that Isaiah 51:12 diagnoses is the anxiety that the risen Christ addresses at every resurrection appearance: do not be afraid (Matthew 28:10; Revelation 1:17).
13: And you have forgotten the LORD, your Maker, who stretched out the heavens and laid the foundations of the earth, and you fear continually all the day because of the wrath of the oppressor, when he sets himself to destroy? And where is the wrath of the oppressor? The diagnostic — forgetting the Creator while fearing the oppressor. Matthew 10:28: do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell. Jesus reorients fear from the human oppressor to the living God — the identical move Isaiah 51:13 makes. The oppressor's wrath is temporary; YHWH's comfort is eternal. The question where is the wrath of the oppressor? anticipates: it will not last.
14: He who is bowed down shall speedily be released; he shall not die and go down to the pit; neither shall his bread fail. The release of the prisoner from death in the pit. Luke 4:18: Jesus reads to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovery of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed — his mission statement. Acts 2:27: Peter quotes Psalms 16:10 of the resurrection: you will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor will you let your Holy One see corruption. Christ himself is the primary fulfillment of he shall not die and go down to the pit — and then, through him, neither will those who are in him (John 11:25-26: whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live).
15: I am the LORD your God, who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar — the LORD of hosts is his name. YHWH who commands the sea — enacted by Jesus in Mark 4:37-39: the storm on the Sea of Galilee, rebuked with peace, be still! The disciples' astonished question: who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him? (Mark 4:41) is answered by Isaiah 51:15: the one who stirs up and calms the sea is the LORD of hosts. Jesus's storm-rebuke is one of the clearest exercises of YHWH's own divine identity in the Gospels — the Creator exercising authority over what he made.
16: And I have put my words in your mouth and covered you in the shadow of my hand, establishing the heavens and laying the foundation of the earth, and saying to Zion, You are my people. The word put in the Servant's mouth — the same image as the sharp sword-mouth of Isaiah 49:2. Hebrews 1:1-2: God who spoke through the prophets has in these last days spoken to us by his Son. John 1:1: in the beginning was the Word. The word placed in the Servant's mouth is ultimately the eternal Word himself — the Son who is both the speaking and the spoken. The shadow of YHWH's hand covering the Servant is the divine protection: John 10:28-29 — no one will snatch them out of my hand... no one can snatch them out of the Father's hand.
17: Wake yourself, wake yourself, stand up, O Jerusalem, you who have drunk from the LORD's hand the cup of his wrath, who have drunk to the dregs the bowl, the cup of staggering. Jerusalem has drunk the cup of YHWH's fury to the dregs — and now is called to wake up. The cup of wrath is the central image of Christ's Gethsemane prayer: Matthew 26:39 — if it is possible, let this cup pass from me. The cup that Jerusalem has drunk is the cup that Christ takes up — drinking the wrath that they deserved so that it may be removed from them (v22). The sleeping/waking language is also resurrection language: Ephesians 5:14 — Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you.
18: There is none to guide her among all the sons she has borne; there is none to take her by the hand among all the sons she has brought up. Jerusalem without a guide or a hand to grasp — utterly alone in her suffering. This is the condition of abandonment that Christ enters in the incarnation: he comes as the guide Israel never had. Hebrews 2:10: it was fitting that he, for whom and by whom all things exist, in bringing many sons to glory, should make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering. The one who brings many sons to glory is the one who takes them by the hand — who holds them in a grip that no one can break (John 10:28).
19: These two things have come upon you — who will grieve for you? — devastation and destruction, famine and sword; who will comfort you? The double disaster — devastation and famine — with no comforter. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4: God is the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction. The absence of human comforters sets up YHWH's own arrival as comforter — and Christ who promises: John 16:33 — in the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world. The one who overcomes the world is the comforter who addresses every possible affliction.
20: Your sons have fainted; they lie at the head of every street like an antelope in a net; they are full of the wrath of the LORD, the rebuke of your God. The sons of Jerusalem fallen under divine rebuke — lying exhausted in the streets. This is the condition of desolation that Christ's lament addresses: Matthew 23:37-38 — O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing! See, your house is left to you desolate. The fainted sons lying in the streets is the desolation that results from rejecting the one who would have gathered them.
21: Therefore hear this, you who are afflicted, who are drunk, but not with wine. The afflicted and drunk — not with wine but with suffering and divine judgment. The first beatitude addresses exactly this condition: Matthew 5:3-4 — blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. The sermon to the afflicted who are drunk on suffering is the Sermon on the Mount — the announcement that the kingdom belongs to precisely those who have nothing left of their own strength.
22: Thus says your Lord, the LORD, your God who pleads the cause of his people: Behold, I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering; the bowl of my fury you shall drink no more. YHWH removes the cup of wrath from Jerusalem's hand — and the mechanism is the cross. The cup that Jesus takes in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:39) is the bowl of divine fury — he drinks it so that his people need drink it no more. Romans 5:9: since we have been justified by his blood, how much more shall we be saved by him from the wrath of God. 1 Thessalonians 1:10: Jesus delivers us from the wrath to come. The cup-removal of Isaiah 51:22 is the atonement accomplished at Calvary.
23: And I will put it into the hand of your tormentors, who have said to you, Bow down, that we may pass over; and you have made your back like the ground and like the street for them to pass over. The cup transferred from the oppressed to the oppressors — the eschatological reversal of all history's humiliations. Revelation 18:6: pay her back as she herself has paid back others, and repay her double for her deeds. The final judgment transfers the cup of wrath to all who enforced the humiliation of YHWH's people. The covenant community whose back was made into a road for the oppressor to walk over (the slave-posture of maximum degradation) is vindicated when the oppressor receives what they gave. The last are first; the humiliated are exalted (Matthew 20:16; Luke 1:52).
